25.4% ([[Nielsen Ratings]]) | last = 76th | next = 78th }} The '''77th Academy Awards''' ceremony, presented by the [[Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences]] (AMPAS), took place on February 27, 2005, at the [[Kodak Theatre]] in [[Hollywood, Los Angeles]] beginning at 5:30 p.m. PST / 8:30 p.m. EST. During the ceremony, AMPAS presented [[Academy Awards]] (commonly referred to as the Oscars) in 24 categories honoring [[2004 in film|films released in 2004]]. The ceremony, televised in the United States by [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]], was produced by [[Gilbert Cates|Gil Cates]] and was directed by Louis J. Horvitz.{{cite news|last1=Feiwell |first1=Jill |title=Familiar face in Oscar seat |url=https://variety.com/2004/film/news/familiar-face-in-oscar-seat-1117911809/ |access-date=July 30, 2014 over 42 million viewers in the United States alone. == Winners and nominees == The nominees for the 77th Academy Awards were announced on January 25, 2005, at the [[Samuel Goldwyn Theater]] in [[Beverly Hills, California]], by [[Frank Pierson]], president of the academy, and actor [[Adrien Brody]].{{cite news|title=Oscar Watch: Brody on board for noms |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150205100712/http://www.nytimes.com/2005/02/28/movies/oscars/28osca.html |archive-date=February 5, 2015 }} At age 74, [[Clint Eastwood]] became the oldest winner for Best Director in Oscar history.{{harvnb|Crouse|2005|p=235}} With his latest unsuccessful nomination for directing ''The Aviator'', nominee [[Martin Scorsese]] joined [[Robert Altman]], [[Clarence Brown]], [[Alfred Hitchcock]], and [[King Vidor]] as the most nominated individuals in the Best Director category without a single win at the time.{{harvnb|Osborne|2008|p=366}} Best Actor winner [[Jamie Foxx]] became the third actor and tenth individual overall to earn [[List of actors nominated for multiple Academy Awards in the same year|two acting nominations in the same year]].{{harvnb|Todd|2008|p=3}} By virtue of her portrayal of [[Katharine Hepburn]], Best Supporting Actress winner [[Cate Blanchett]] was the first performer to win an Academy Award for a portrayal of a previous Academy Award winner.{{harvnb|Osborne|2008|p=368}} "[[Al otro lado del río]]" from ''[[The Motorcycle Diaries (film)|The Motorcycle Diaries]]'' became the second song with non-English lyrics to win Best Original Song. The first to achieve this feat was the [[Never on Sunday (song)|titular song]] from the 1960 Greek film ''[[Never on Sunday]]'' at the [[33rd Academy Awards]].{{cite news|last1=Gray |first1=Tim |title=Oscar's fistful of 'Dollar' |url=https://variety.com/2005/film/news/oscar-s-fistful-of-dollar-1117918632/ |access-date=July 30, 2014 |work=Variety |publisher=PMC |date=February 27, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140812201842/http://variety.com/2005/film/news/oscar-s-fistful-of-dollar-1117918632/ |archive-date=August 12, 2014 }} ===Awards=== [[File:Clint Eastwood at 2010 New York Film Festival.jpg|thumb|right|150px|[[Clint Eastwood]], Best Director winner and Best Picture co-winner]] [[File:Jamie Foxx by Gage Skidmore.jpg|thumb|right|150px|[[Jamie Foxx]], Best Actor winner]] [[File:Life Ball 2013 - Beyoncé | Performers || "[[Believe (Josh Groban song)|Believe]]" from ''[[The Polar Express (film)|The Polar Express]]'' |} == Ceremony information == [[File:Chris Rock WE 2012 Shankbone.JPG|thumb|130px|right|[[Chris Rock]] hosted the 77th Academy Awards|alt=Photo of Chris Rock attending the premiere of the 2012 film ''What to Expect When Your Expecting''.]] Opting for a younger face in an attempt to increase viewership, while renewing interest with the nominated films, producer [[Gil Cates]] selected actor and comedian [[Chris Rock]] to host the 2005 ceremony. Cates explained his decision to hire Rock for the telecast in a press release saying, "I am a huge fan of Chris Rock. He always makes me laugh and he always has something interesting to say. Chris represents the best of the new generation of comics. Having him host the Oscars is terrific. I can't wait."{{cite news |title=Chris Rock to Host Oscars |url=https://www.foxnews.com/story/chris-rock-to-host-oscars/ |access-date=July 30, 2014 |work=Fox News |publisher=21st Century Fox |date=October 14, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140812125137/http://www.foxnews.com/story/2004/10/14/chris-rock-to-host-oscars/ |archive-date=2014-08-12 }} By virtue of his selection, Rock became the first [[African American]] man to solo host the gala.{{cite news|last1=McNamara |first1=Mary |title=Rock On |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2005-feb-27-tm-rock09-story.html |access-date=July 14, 2014 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=February 27, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140812194715/http://articles.latimes.com/2005/feb/27/magazine/tm-rock09 |archive-date=August 12, 2014 }} Nearly a month before the ceremony Rock told Josh Wolk of ''[[Entertainment Weekly]]'', "Come on, it's a fashion show. No one performs; it's not like a music show. What straight black man sits there and watches the Oscars? Show me one."{{cite magazine|last1=Wolk |first1=Josh |title=The Oscars Get Rock-ed |url=https://ew.com/article/2005/02/01/chris-rock-talks-about-his-first-oscar-hosting-gig/ |access-date=August 1, 2014 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Warner |date=February 1, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090421033356/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C1021799%2C00.html |archive-date=April 21, 2009 }} Political blogger [[Matt Drudge]] later reported that several anonymous AMPAS members wanted Rock fired from his hosting job as a result of the comments.{{cite news|last1=Grossberg |first1=Josh |title=Rock Roiling Oscars |url=http://www.eonline.com/news/49247/rock-roiling-oscars |access-date=August 1, 2014 |work=E! |publisher=NBCUniversal |date=February 15, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140811131131/http://www.eonline.com/news/49247/rock-roiling-oscars |archive-date=August 11, 2014 }} Nevertheless, producer Cates issued a statement defending the host saying, "Chris' comments are meant to be humorous digs at a show that some people, obviously including Chris himself, think may be a bit too stuffy."{{cite news |title=Producer supports Chris Rock as show's host |url=http://old.chronicle.augusta.com/stories/2005/02/16/osc_443191.shtml |access-date=August 1, 2014 |work=The Augusta Chronicle |publisher=Morris Communications |date=February 16, 2005 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808051558/http://old.chronicle.augusta.com/stories/2005/02/16/osc_443191.shtml |archive-date=2014-08-08 }} Furthermore, Wolk dismissed any controversy regarding Rock's comments and that Drudge exaggerated the host's comments.{{cite magazine|last1=Wolk |first1=Josh |title=Rock and Roll |url=https://ew.com/article/2005/02/18/ews-chris-rock-interviewer-weighs-matt-drudge/ |access-date=August 1, 2014 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Warner |date=February 18, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100412230426/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C1029468%2C00.html |archive-date=April 12, 2010 }} [[GLAAD]] Executive Director Joan Garry also issue a statement in light of the controversy stating, "Chris Rock isn't making fun of gays – he's poking fun at the Oscars."{{cite news|last1=Britt |first1=Donna |title=Drudge Takes the Oscar for 'Clueless' |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/articles/A33625-2005Feb17.html |access-date=June 14, 2014 |newspaper=The Washington Post |date=February 18, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150205092152/http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/articles/A33625-2005Feb17.html |archive-date=February 5, 2015 }} Rock appeared on ''[[The Tonight Show with Jay Leno]]'' the Monday before the ceremony to clarify his comments. When [[Jay Leno|Leno]] asked about the statement, Rock replied "I did not say that. I said only gay people watch the [[Tony Award|Tony]]s." However, he reiterated, "I really don't know any straight men who aren't in show business that have ever watched the Oscars."{{cite news|last1=Moloshok |first1=Danny |title=Chris Rock clarifies Oscar comments |url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2005-02-22-rock-oscar-comments_x.htm |access-date=August 1, 2014 |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |date=February 22, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808123023/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2005-02-22-rock-oscar-comments_x.htm |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} Notable changes were made to give the ceremony a sleek, interactive look while shortening the length of the ceremony. Cates announced that in certain categories, all five nominees would be up onstage prior to the announcement of the award.{{cite news|last1=Schodolski |first1=Vincent J. |title=Hipper, shorter, edgier Oscars: Ceremony undergoes nips/tucks |url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2005/02/17/hipper-shorter-edgier-oscars-ceremony-undergoes-nipstucks/ |access-date=August 1, 2014 |work=Chicago Tribune |publisher=Tribune Company |date=February 17, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808052808/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2005-02-17/features/0502170055_1_nominees-77th-academy-awards-gil-cates |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} In other instances, the actor or actress would present the award in the audience.{{cite news|last1=Keller |first1=Julie |title=Oscars Timely Makeover |url=http://www.eonline.com/news/49191/oscar-s-timely-makeovers |access-date=September 18, 2014 |work=E! |publisher=NBCUniversal |date=February 8, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140811131125/http://www.eonline.com/news/49191/oscar-s-timely-makeovers |archive-date=August 11, 2014 }} In addition, production designer Roy Christopher designed a technologically ambitious stage for the telecast that both saluted the past while look toward the future. The set prominently featured 26 [[high-definition video]] monitors floating over the first twelve rows of the audience and a 40-foot [[Light-emitting diode|LED]] screen situated beneath a layer of plexiglass on the stage floor.{{cite news |title=Oscar set will blur line between performers, audience |url=http://old.chronicle.augusta.com/stories/021905/osc_set.shtml |access-date=July 14, 2014 |work=The Augusta Chronicle |publisher=Morris Communications |date=February 17, 2005 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808051242/http://old.chronicle.augusta.com/stories/021905/osc_set.shtml |archive-date=2014-08-08 }} Both screens were used display images of previous Oscar appearances as presenters took the stage or random film clips during several commercial breaks.{{cite news|title=Oscars take on an interactive feel |url=http://www.today.com/id/6989524/ns/today-today_entertainment/t/oscars-take-interactive-feel/ |access-date=July 14, 2014 |work=NBC News |publisher=NBCUniversal |date=February 17, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808062757/http://www.today.com/id/6989524/ns/today-today_entertainment/t/oscars-take-interactive-feel/ |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} A gold rod featuring 23 different life-sized Oscar statuettes spiraling upward was placed at center stage.{{cite news|last1=Bowles |first1=Scott |title=Look up and look out: Academy Awards get ready to rock |url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2005-02-17-oscar-stage_x.htm |access-date=September 11, 2013 |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |date=February 17, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150207100117/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2005-02-17-oscar-stage_x.htm |archive-date=February 7, 2015 }} Several other people were involved with the production of the ceremony. Film composer and musician [[Bill Conti]] served as musical director of the ceremony.{{cite news|last1=Thompson |first1=Jenn |title=Oscarcast vets return |url=https://variety.com/2004/film/news/oscarcast-vets-return-1117915000/ |access-date=July 30, 2014 |work=Variety |publisher=PMC |date=December 14, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140812201903/http://variety.com/2004/film/news/oscarcast-vets-return-1117915000/ |archive-date=August 12, 2014 }} AMPAS graphics designer Brett Davidson designed the official ceremony poster consisting of a profile of the Oscar statuette in front of four neon-colored squares.{{cite news|last1=Feiwell |first1=Jill |title=Acad drawn to staffer's design |url=https://variety.com/2004/film/news/acad-drawn-to-staffer-s-design-1117913624/ |access-date=July 31, 2014 |work=Variety |publisher=PMC |date=November 16, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140812201922/http://variety.com/2004/film/news/acad-drawn-to-staffer-s-design-1117913624/ |archive-date=August 12, 2014 }} Freelance producer Cochise and media firm Dig and Media Island released a trailer shown in movie theaters nationwide promoting the ceremony featuring clips from past Oscar ceremonies against the four squares backdrop in the aforementioned poster. The trailer featured the song "[[Hey Mama (The Black Eyed Peas song)|Hey Mama]]" by [[The Black Eyed Peas]].{{cite news|last1=Chang |first1=Justin |title=Oscars hitched to trailer |url=https://variety.com/2005/film/news/oscars-hitched-to-trailer-1117916390/ |access-date=July 31, 2014 |work=Variety |publisher=PMC |date=January 16, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140812201942/https://variety.com/2005/film/news/oscars-hitched-to-trailer-1117916390/ |archive-date=August 12, 2014 }} Two-time Oscar-winning actor [[Dustin Hoffman]] narrated the opening montage highlighting the evolution of the movies.{{cite news|last1=Chaney |first1=Jen |title=Oscars Live |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/articles/A48016-2005Feb23.html |access-date=July 31, 2014 |newspaper=The Washington Post |date=February 27, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141011122320/http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/articles/A48016-2005Feb23.html |archive-date=October 11, 2014 }} ===Box office performance of nominated films=== When the nominations were announced on January 25, the field of Best Picture nominees did not include a bona fide blockbuster at the U.S. [[box office]]. It was the first time since 1986 that none of the five films in that category were among the top ten releases in box office prior to the nominations announcement.{{cite news|last1=Gray |first1=Brandon |title='Aviator,' 'Million Dollar Baby' Get Best Picture Boost |url=https://www.boxofficemojo.com/news/?id=1670&p=.htm |access-date=July 31, 2014 |work=Box Office Mojo |date=January 31, 2014 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140715104358/http://boxofficemojo.com/news/?id=1670&p=.htm |archive-date=July 15, 2014 }} Furthermore, before the ceremony, all five films sold the lowest cumulative number of tickets sold since 1984.{{cite news |title=Ticket sales are slow for best-picture nominees |url=http://old.chronicle.augusta.com/stories/2005/02/17/osc_443399.shtml |access-date=July 31, 2014 |work=The August Chronicle |publisher=Morris Communications |date=February 17, 2005 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808051244/http://old.chronicle.augusta.com/stories/2005/02/17/osc_443399.shtml |archive-date=2014-08-08 }} ''Ray'' was the highest-grossing film among the Best Picture nominees with $73 million in domestic box office receipts. The film was followed by ''The Aviator'' ($58.4 million), ''Finding Neverland'' ($32.7 million), ''Sideways'' ($32.4 million), and finally ''Million Dollar Baby'' ($8.4 million).{{cite news|title=2004 Academy Award Nominations and Winner for Best Picture |url=https://www.boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=2004&p=.htm |work=[[Box Office Mojo]] |access-date=July 14, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130809030345/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=2004&p=.htm |archive-date=August 9, 2013 }} The combined gross of the five Best Picture nominees when the Oscars were announced was $205 million with an average gross of $41.3 million per film. Among the rest of the top 50 releases of 2004 in U.S. box office before the nominations, 44 nominations went to 14 films on the list. Only ''Shrek 2'' (1st), ''The Incredibles'' (4th), ''Shark Tale'' (11th), ''Collateral'' (22nd), ''Ray'' (37th), and ''The Aviator'' (49th) were nominated for Best Picture, Best Animated Feature, directing, acting, or screenwriting.{{cite news|url=https://www.boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2005-01-24&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=2004&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |title=2004 Yearly Box Office Results (January 24, 2005) |work=Box Office Mojo |access-date=July 14, 2014 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808053502/http://www.boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2005-01-24&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=2004&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} The other top 50 box office hits that earned the nominations were ''Spider-Man 2'' (2nd), ''The Passion of the Christ'' (3rd), ''Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban'' (5th), ''The Polar Express'' (10th), ''I, Robot'' (12th), ''Troy'' (13th), ''Lemony Snicket's A Series of Unfortunate Events'' (18th), and ''The Village'' (20th). ===Jude Law joke=== During his monologue, host Rock joked, "[[Clint Eastwood]]'s a star, OK? [[Tobey Maguire]]'s just a boy in tights," He also added, "You want [[Tom Cruise]] and all you can get is [[Jude Law]]? Wait. You want [[Russell Crowe]] and all you can get is [[Colin Farrell]]? Wait. ''[[Alexander (2004 film)|Alexander]]'' is not ''[[Gladiator (2000 film)|Gladiator]]''." In response, Sean Penn rebutted Rock's remarks praising Law as one of his generation's "finest actors".{{cite news|title=Host Chris Rock comes out swinging |url=http://www.today.com/id/7042377/ns/today-today_entertainment/t/host-chris-rock-comes-out-swinging/ |access-date=August 1, 2014 |work=NBC News |publisher=NBC Universal |date=February 28, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150212170804/http://www.today.com/id/7042377/ns/today-today_entertainment/t/host-chris-rock-comes-out-swinging/ |archive-date=February 12, 2015 }} Over a year later, Law expressed his anger toward Rock in ''[[The New York Times]]'' telling columnist Craig Modderno, "At first I laughed because I didn't think he knew who I was. Then I got angry as his remarks became personal. My friends were livid. It's unfortunate I had five or six films come out at the same time."{{cite news|last1=Modderno |first1=Craig |title=Jude Law Is an Actor in Demand |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2006/09/03/movies/03modd.html |access-date=August 1, 2014 |work=The New York Times |date=September 3, 2006 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150212170619/http://www.nytimes.com/2006/09/03/movies/03modd.html |archive-date=February 12, 2015 }} ===Scrapped Robin Williams song=== [[Robin Williams]] initially wanted to sing a humorous song written by [[Marc Shaiman]] and [[Scott Wittman]] during the presentation of the Best Animated Feature award satirizing the controversy regarding [[Focus on the Family]] and a music video sponsored by [[We Are Family Foundation]] featuring animated characters such as [[SpongeBob SquarePants (character)|SpongeBob SquarePants]] singing the song "[[We Are Family (song)|We Are Family]]". The song contained lyrics such as "[[Pinocchio (1940 film)#Voice cast|Pinocchio]]'s had his nose done! [[Aurora (Sleeping Beauty)|Sleeping Beauty]] is popping pills!/ The [[Three Little Pigs (film)|Three Little Pigs]] ain't kosher! [[Betty Boop]] works Beverly Hills!"{{cite magazine|last1=Shaiman |first1=Marc |title=Robin Williams' Oscar Gag |url=https://www.ew.com/ew/article/0,,1033999,00.html |access-date=August 1, 2014 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Warner |date=March 7, 2005 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808050539/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C1033999%2C00.html |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} However, Cates and ABC officials deemed the song overly vulgar and offensive for the telecast and was dropped altogether after writers Shaiman and Wittman had trouble rewriting more appropriate lyrics.{{cite news|last1=Halbfinger |first1=David M. |title=Cut From the Oscars: Cartoon Characters' Sins |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2005/02/27/movies/oscars/27williams.html |access-date=August 1, 2014 |work=The New York Times |date=February 27, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140811012558/http://www.nytimes.com/2005/02/27/movies/oscars/27williams.html |archive-date=August 11, 2014 }} Williams eventually presented the Best Animated Feature award as scheduled, but silently mocked the debacle by entering the stage with duct tape over his mouth before speaking.{{cite news|last1=Wloszczyna |first1=Susan |title=Williams will be heard but not seen |url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/news/2005-03-06-robin-williams-main_x.htm |access-date=August 1, 2014 |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |date=March 7, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808123030/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/news/2005-03-06-robin-williams-main_x.htm |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} ===Critical reviews=== The show received a mixed reception from media publications. Some media outlets were more critical of the show and Rock's performance as host. ''[[USA Today]]'' television critic Robert Bianco wrote, "Loud, snide and dismissive, he wasn't just a disappointment; he ranks up there with the worst hosts ever." He also called the decision to have several nominees of several technical categories stand on stage embarrassing and disrespectful.{{cite news|last1=Bianco |first1=Robert |title=Alas, format did not do Oscar proud |url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/television/news/2005-02-28-bianco-review_x.htm |access-date=July 14, 2014 |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |date=February 28, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808052352/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/television/news/2005-02-28-bianco-review_x.htm |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} Columnist Robert. P. Lawrence of the ''[[San Diego Union Tribune]]'' commented, "It was a frustratingly average, three-hour-12-minute exhibition of mutual admiration in the inimitable Hollywood style." He later said that despite Rock's edgy and provocative opening, his humor and energy diminished as the night wore on.{{cite news|last1=Lawrence |first1=Robert P. |title=Even Chris Rock came off as tepid and tame at the Oscars |url=http://www.utsandiego.com/uniontrib/20050228/news_1c28laurencm.html |access-date=July 31, 2014 |work=[[San Diego Union Tribune]] |publisher=Tribune Publishing |date=February 28, 2005 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808043630/http://www.utsandiego.com/uniontrib/20050228/news_1c28laurencm.html |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} Vince Horiuchi of ''[[The Salt Lake Tribune]]'' wrote of Rock's performance, "He was bound by stale jokes (none of the winners "tested positive for steroids"), a rigid opening monologue (he didn't even make reference to his prior controversial comments about the Oscars), and tired comedy bits (Rock playing like [[Catherine Zeta-Jones]] with [[Adam Sandler]])." He also described the cast and production of the ceremony as "moribund" and "clumsy".{{cite news|last1=Horiuchi|first1=Vincent|title=If they give Oscars for boring, this year's broadcast wins big|work=The Salt Lake Tribune|publisher=MediaNew Group|date=February 28, 2005|page=C7}} Other media outlets received the broadcast more positively. Film critic [[Roger Ebert]] noted that Rock "opened on a high-energy quick-talking note" He also added, "Chris Rock hit a home run with his opening monologue, which was surprisingly pointed, topical, and not shy of controversy."{{cite news|last1=Ebert |first1=Roger |title='Baby' Stages Late-Round Oscar Rally |url=https://www.rogerebert.com/festivals-and-awards/baby-stages-late-round-oscar-rally |access-date=July 31, 2014 |work=Chicago Sun-Times |publisher=Sun-Times Media Group |date=February 27, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140811005537/http://www.rogerebert.com/festivals-and-awards/baby-stages-late-round-oscar-rally |archive-date=August 11, 2014 }} Television critic [[Frazier Moore]] commented that Rock's performance was a "needed pick-me-up, presiding over the broadcast with saucy finesse." He added, "In sum, the broadcast felt brisk, though not rushed. It felt modern and refreshingly free of chronic self-importance."{{cite news |last1=Moore |first1=Frazier |title=Breezy pace scores a win for the Oscars |url=http://alb.merlinone.net/mweb/wmsql.wm.request?oneimage&imageid=6325353 |access-date=July 31, 2014 |work=Times Union |publisher=Hearst Corporation |date=March 1, 2005 |agency=Associated Press |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20140806062043/http://alb.merlinone.net/mweb/wmsql.wm.request?oneimage&imageid=6325353 |archive-date=August 6, 2014 }} Brian Lowry of ''[[Variety (magazine)|Variety]]'' gave an average review of Rock but remarked, "For all the hand-wringing about the awards descending into the muck, the 77th Academy Awards proved a classy affair, with precious little red meat to satiate Hollywood bashers." ===Ratings and reception=== The American telecast on ABC drew an average of 42.14 million people over its length, which was a 3% decrease from the [[76th Academy Awards|previous year's ceremony]].{{cite news|url=https://variety.com/2005/scene/news/fox-idol-find-sweeps-groove-1117918745/ |title=Fox, 'Idol' find sweeps groove |last=Kiseell |first=Rick |date=March 2, 2005 |work=Variety |publisher=PMC |access-date=July 30, 2014 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140812201858/http://variety.com/2005/scene/news/fox-idol-find-sweeps-groove-1117918745/ |archive-date=August 12, 2014 }} The show also drew lower [[Nielsen ratings]] compared to the two previous ceremonies with 25.4 of households watching over a 38 share.{{cite news|last1=Atkinson |first1=Claire |title=Final Oscar Ratings Contradict Early Reports |url=http://adage.com/article/media/final-oscar-tv-ratings-contradict-early-reports/45224/ |access-date=February 6, 2015 |work=[[Advertising Age]] |publisher=[[Crain Communications]] |date=March 1, 2005 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150205112245/http://adage.com/article/media/final-oscar-tv-ratings-contradict-early-reports/45224/ |archive-date=February 5, 2015 }} It also drew a lower 18–49 demo rating with a 15.1 rating over a 34 share among viewers in that demographic. =="In Memoriam"== The annual "In Memoriam" tribute was presented by actress [[Annette
